title: Should cities slow down their public spaces?
summary: Argue whether cities should deliberately design streets and squares for slowness rather than speed.
topic: Whether cities should slow down their public spaces
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) on whether cities should slow down their public spaces — pedestrianised zones, 20 km/h limits, fewer through-roads. Use a concession ('Granted, …'), a cleft sentence, and at least one international example. Take a clear side.
hintStarters: When Pontevedra closed its old centre to cars in 1999…
What slowness actually buys a city is…
Granted, the case for speed has its own logic.
It seems to me that the deeper question is…
sampleText:
When Pontevedra, a small city in northwestern Spain, closed its old centre to through-traffic in 1999, the prediction was straightforward: shops would die, residents would flee, and the experiment would last a season. None of that happened. Footfall rose, child road deaths fell to zero, and a generation of children grew up treating the carriageway as a place to walk rather than a place to fear. Pontevedra has since become the standard reference for any city wondering whether to slow itself down on purpose.

What slowness actually buys a city is not nostalgia but attention. A street designed for thirty kilometres an hour invites you to look up at a balcony, to recognise a neighbour, to linger over a coffee that you would otherwise drink standing. Speed, by contrast, tends to flatten everything it passes — shopfronts become blurs, faces become obstacles. It is no accident that the streets people remember from their childhoods are almost never the wide ones.

Granted, the case for speed has its own logic. Ambulances need clear lanes; delivery vehicles keep shops alive; commuters who already give an hour to traffic do not want to give ninety minutes. These are real costs, not imagined ones, and any honest argument for slowness has to make room for them. What slow-city advocates often miss is that not every street can become a square; some have to remain arteries so the others can become rooms.

Even so, the deeper question is what kind of public life a city wants to be possible. Ljubljana pedestrianised its centre in 2007 and saw the same pattern as Pontevedra: more children outdoors, fewer cars, perceptibly more conversation between strangers. What's more, the spillover into side streets has not been the chaos critics warned of; drivers, given fewer easy options, simply make fewer trips. Slowness, it turns out, is partly a way of asking people to plan.

This is partly because cities have spent a century optimising for the car and only recently begun to count what was lost. A bench is not a substitute for a road, but a road without benches is not really a place — it is a corridor between places. If a city slows down a square, it is not punishing drivers; it is admitting that some streets should belong, at least in the daytime, to the people who live above them rather than the people passing through.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft for thematic emphasis
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What slowness actually buys a city is not nostalgia but attention' đặt điều quan trọng nhất (attention) ở cuối câu để nhấn mạnh.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What slowness actually buys a city is not nostalgia but attention' uses a what-cleft to delay the key noun until the very end of the sentence.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Concession with 'Granted, … Even so, …'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Cấu trúc 'Granted, the case for speed has its own logic… Even so, the deeper question is…' thừa nhận phía đối lập trước khi quay lại lập luận chính.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Granted, … Even so, …' is a B2 concession pattern: admit the opposing view, then return to your own line of argument.

sampleTranslationVi:
Khi Pontevedra, một thành phố nhỏ ở tây bắc Tây Ban Nha, đóng cửa trung tâm cũ với lưu thông xuyên qua vào năm 1999, dự đoán là đơn giản: cửa hàng sẽ chết, cư dân sẽ rời đi, và thí nghiệm chỉ kéo dài một mùa. Không điều nào trong số đó xảy ra. Lượng người qua lại tăng, số trẻ em chết vì tai nạn giao thông giảm về không, và một thế hệ trẻ em lớn lên coi lòng đường là nơi để đi bộ chứ không phải nơi để sợ. Pontevedra từ đó trở thành tham chiếu chuẩn cho bất kỳ thành phố nào tự hỏi có nên chậm lại một cách có chủ ý hay không.

Điều mà sự chậm rãi thật sự mang lại cho một thành phố không phải là hoài niệm mà là sự chú ý. Một con đường được thiết kế cho tốc độ ba mươi cây số một giờ mời gọi bạn ngước nhìn ban công, nhận ra một người hàng xóm, nán lại bên ly cà phê mà bạn sẽ uống đứng nếu vội. Tốc độ, ngược lại, có xu hướng làm phẳng mọi thứ nó đi qua — cửa hiệu trở thành những vệt mờ, khuôn mặt trở thành chướng ngại. Không phải ngẫu nhiên khi những con phố mà người ta nhớ từ tuổi thơ hầu như không bao giờ là những con phố rộng.

Công nhận, lập luận ủng hộ tốc độ có lý của riêng nó. Xe cứu thương cần làn đường thông thoáng; xe giao hàng giữ cho cửa tiệm sống; người đi làm đã mất một giờ trong kẹt xe không muốn mất chín mươi phút. Đó là những chi phí thật, không phải tưởng tượng, và một lập luận trung thực cho sự chậm rãi phải nhường chỗ cho chúng. Điều mà những người ủng hộ thành phố chậm thường bỏ sót là không phải con đường nào cũng có thể trở thành quảng trường; một số phải vẫn là trục chính để những con khác có thể trở thành phòng khách.

Dẫu vậy, câu hỏi sâu hơn là thành phố muốn loại đời sống công cộng nào là khả thể. Ljubljana đã biến trung tâm thành phố đi bộ vào năm 2007 và chứng kiến cùng một quy luật: nhiều trẻ em ngoài trời hơn, ít xe hơn, rõ ràng nhiều cuộc trò chuyện giữa người lạ hơn. Hơn nữa, tác động lan tỏa sang các phố nhỏ không phải là sự hỗn loạn mà những người chỉ trích cảnh báo; người lái xe, khi có ít lựa chọn dễ dàng hơn, đơn giản là đi ít chuyến hơn. Sự chậm rãi, hóa ra, một phần là cách yêu cầu mọi người lập kế hoạch.

Điều này một phần là vì các thành phố đã dành một thế kỷ để tối ưu cho ô tô và chỉ gần đây bắt đầu đếm những gì đã mất. Một chiếc ghế băng không thay thế được một con đường, nhưng một con đường không có ghế băng không thật sự là một nơi — nó là một hành lang giữa các nơi. Nếu một thành phố làm chậm một quảng trường, nó không phải đang trừng phạt người lái xe; nó đang thừa nhận rằng một số con phố nên thuộc về, ít nhất là vào ban ngày, những người sống phía trên chúng hơn là những người chỉ đi ngang qua.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft most naturally to put the key idea at the end?
options: What slowness actually buys a city is not nostalgia but attention.
Slowness actually what buys is the city not nostalgia.
It is what slowness buys that not the nostalgia attention.
Slowness buys what the city is not nostalgia but attention.
mcQuestions.explanation: The pattern 'What X does is Y' delays the key noun (Y) until the end for emphasis.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option introduces a concession most naturally?
options: Granted, the case for speed has its own logic.
Even though granted speed logic has own.
Of course granted speed it is logical the case.
Conceding which speed has logic its own granted.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' is a clean B2 concession opener that admits the opposing side.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the simplest, most natural opener that admits the other view.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses hedging language most naturally?
options: Speed definitely 100% flattens every single thing it passes always.
Speed tends to flatten everything it passes.
Speed is flattening surely all things which passes it.
Speed must always being flatten all the thing it pass.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tends to' hedges the claim, which is more credible at B2 than absolute statements.
mcQuestions.hint: Which one is the most careful / least over-stated?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best continues a paragraph that ended with a concrete example from Ljubljana?
options: Throughout history, all cities have always benefited from slowing down.
What's more, the spillover into side streets has not been the chaos critics warned of.
In conclusion, slow cities are very good and important.
It is important to note that cities are made of streets.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What's more, …' adds a related cohesive point and stays specific. The other options are AI-style filler.
mcQuestions.hint: Which one adds detail rather than restates a platitude?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which cleft structure puts the focus on the people slow-city advocates overlook?
options: What slow-city advocates often miss is that not every street can become a square.
It is miss what slow-city advocates often that the street become a square.
Slow-city advocates miss what often the street is a square.
What is missed slow-city often advocates square street.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What X miss is that …' is a valid what-cleft fronting the overlooked content.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option keeps the register of a B2 argumentative essay?
options: Cars are super annoying lol and slow streets r way better.
A street designed for thirty kilometres an hour invites you to look up at a balcony.
If u walk slow u will see balcony!!!
Slow streets are like, you know, basically nicer or whatever.
mcQuestions.explanation: Only option B keeps a formal, considered tone with a specific image.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'tends to' as a hedge correctly?
options: Tends to speed flatten everything it passing by.
Speed tends to flatten everything it passes.
Speed is tend to flatten all the thing.
Speed tend flattens everything that pass by.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Speed tends to + base verb' is the correct hedging structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which collocation is most natural with 'public'?
options: public corridor
public footfall
public life
public arteries
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Public life' is a fixed collocation meaning civic activity in shared space.
mcQuestions.hint: Which two words go together most often?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'It is no accident that' correctly to imply causation?
options: It is no accident that the streets people remember from their childhoods are almost never the wide ones.
It is no accident the streets people remembering wide ones almost never.
Accident no it is which the streets wide remember never.
No accident it that wide streets remember childhood almost.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is no accident that + clause' is the standard pattern implying the result was caused, not coincidental.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the cleanest 'It is no accident that + subject + verb' pattern.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals that the writer is returning to their main argument after a concession?
options: Anyway, moving on,
Even so, the deeper question is…
But whatever, the question is…
On the contrary slash however moreover,
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' acknowledges what was just conceded and pivots back to the writer's position.
mcQuestions.hint: Which one acknowledges and pivots cleanly?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence avoids AI-style cliché in its opening?
options: In today's fast-paced world, cities are too fast.
Throughout history, humans have walked.
When Pontevedra closed its old centre to through-traffic in 1999, the prediction was straightforward.
It is important to note that walking is important.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C opens with a specific time, place, and event, which is concrete rather than generic.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a relative clause correctly?
options: Commuters who already give an hour to traffic do not want to give ninety minutes.
Commuters that already an hour to traffic give they not want ninety minutes.
Commuters which give an hour traffic don't want ninety minute.
Commuters they give an hour to traffic already do not want ninety.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Commuters who already give …' is a clean defining relative clause for people.
mcQuestions.hint: Pick the option with 'who' attaching a defining clause cleanly.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'partly because' correctly to give a partial explanation?
options: Because partly cities a century have optimised for the car only.
This is partly because cities have spent a century optimising for the car.
Partly is because cities a century the car optimising spent.
It is because partly the century cities have car optimised.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'This is partly because + clause' offers one of several reasons and is standard B2 cohesion.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'This is partly because + subject + verb'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses participle-clause syntax correctly?
options: Drivers, given fewer easy options, simply make fewer trips.
Drivers given that fewer options easy, they making fewer trips simply.
Drivers having give fewer options simply they make trips fewer.
Given fewer drivers easy options trip make simply they.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Drivers, given fewer easy options, …' uses a reduced participle clause as a parenthetical.
mcQuestions.hint: Find the option where the participle clause sits cleanly between commas.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which ending avoids moralising and gives a concrete final image?
options: We must all learn the value of slowness in our daily lives.
Slow cities are better cities, and that is the truth.
If a city slows down a square, it is not punishing drivers; it is admitting that some streets should belong, at least in the daytime, to the people who live above them.
Throughout history, cities have always struggled with speed.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C is concrete, specific, and stays inside the argument rather than preaching.
mcQuestions.hint: Pick the one that ends with a specific image, not a sermon.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Pick one street you walk regularly. If a planner halved its speed limit and added benches, who would gain and who would lose — and would the trade be worth it?
